subject: AP US Government and Politics
theme: Americans' political beliefs are shaped by socialization, demographics, and ideology — these beliefs translate into distinct partisan coalitions and policy preferences that drive conflict over the proper role of government in economic and social life.
core_concepts: Political socialization is the lifelong process by which individuals develop political values and beliefs — the primary agents are family (most influential), schools, peers, media, religion, and direct political experience.

The political ideology spectrum ranges from liberal (favor active government, social equality, economic regulation) to conservative (favor limited government, traditional values, free markets) with libertarians and populists as variants.

Public opinion is measured through scientific polling using random sampling — key concepts include sampling error, margin of error, question wording effects, push polls, and the difference between tracking polls and benchmark polls.

Party platforms reflect systematic ideological differences: Democrats generally favor progressive taxation, social safety nets, environmental regulation, and expanded civil rights; Republicans favor lower taxes, deregulation, traditional values, and limited government.

Political efficacy — the belief that one's participation can make a difference — is a key determinant of political engagement; internal efficacy (confidence in one's understanding) and external efficacy (belief the system responds) predict participation.

Generational effects, period effects, and lifecycle effects all shape political opinions — the cohort that comes of age during major political events (Great Depression, Vietnam, 9/11) often develops distinctive political identities.

The 'gender gap,' 'racial gap,' and 'education gap' in party identification show that demographic factors systematically shape political behavior — women, racial minorities, and college graduates lean Democratic; white men without college degrees lean Republican.

Ideology and economic policy: liberals favor Keynesian demand stimulus and progressive taxation; conservatives favor supply-side economics (lower taxes, deregulation). Ideology and social policy divides focus on abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun control, and immigration.

key_terms.definition: A coherent set of beliefs about the proper role of government and the best policies to achieve social and economic goals.
Ideological position favoring active government to promote social equality, environmental protection, economic regulation, and expanded civil rights.
Ideological position favoring limited government, free markets, traditional social values, strong national defense, and skepticism of rapid social change.
Ideology favoring minimal government in both economic and personal matters — supports free markets and civil liberties but opposes government regulation of either.
The lifelong process through which individuals acquire political beliefs, values, and identities — primarily through family, schools, peers, religion, and media.
Institutions and groups that shape political beliefs — family is most important, followed by schools, peers, media, religion, and political events.
The distribution of the population's views on political issues, leaders, institutions, and policies — measured through scientific surveys.
Survey method in which every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected — essential for a sample to be representative of the population.
The statistical uncertainty inherent in surveys of a sample rather than the entire population — typically expressed as a margin of error (±X%).
The range within which the true population value likely falls — a poll showing 52% support with ±3% margin means true support is between 49-55%.
A biased survey designed to influence respondents' opinions rather than measure them — asks leading questions that presuppose negative information about a candidate.
Citizens' belief that they can understand and influence the political system — internal (personal competence) and external (system responsiveness) components predict participation.
The consistent difference in political preferences between men and women — since 1980, women have voted more Democratic than men by 7-12 percentage points.
A formal statement of a party's principles, values, and policy positions — the individual policy commitments within a platform are called 'planks.'
Economic theory associated with John Maynard Keynes advocating government deficit spending during recessions to stimulate demand and restore growth.
Economic theory holding that tax cuts and deregulation for producers stimulate investment, growth, and job creation — associated with Reagan-era conservatism.
Government programs protecting citizens from economic hardship — includes Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid, and unemployment insurance; debates center on size, eligibility, and funding.

Lasting political attitudes formed when a cohort comes of age during formative political events — Depression-era cohort became strong New Deal Democrats, 9/11 generation developed security-focused views.

exam_tip: AP exam MCQs on Unit 4 frequently present polling data, graphs of public opinion trends, or ideological distribution charts — practice reading and interpreting data charts showing party identification, ideological self-placement, and demographic gaps in political preferences.
